title: Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years
subtitle: The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.
caption:
Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years, United States

The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.

This line chart tracks the weekly number of deaths in the United States from early 2020 through early 2021, comparing actual reported figures against a baseline projection derived from 2015–2019 data. The vertical axis measures the raw number of deaths, ranging from 0 to 100,000, while the horizontal axis spans from January 5, 2020, to January 3, 2021. The projection for 2020 is shown as a black line, hovering steadily around 55,000 to 60,000 deaths per week. Actual death counts for 2020, represented by an orange line, diverge significantly from this baseline, peaking above 80,000 in late April and again rising past 80,000 by the end of the year. The purple line for 2021 begins near 85,000 in early January before declining. Lines for 2022 and 2023 remain closer to the historical baseline, generally fluctuating between 55,000 and 65,000. Historical data for 2015 through 2019 appear as grey lines at the bottom of the chart, consistently staying below the 60,000 mark. The reported number of deaths might not count all deaths that occurred due to incomplete coverage and delays in reporting. The most prominent feature of the graph is the sustained elevation of the 2020 and early 2021 death counts well above the projected baseline and historical averages.
